agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Integration request to couple Sales/Order Management with Warehouse Management for real-time stock validation at order placement. This fundamentally changes the current design where orders are placed independently of warehouse state, introducing cross-context coupling with significant domain implications.","bounded_context":"Cross-cutting concern spanning Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B). The feature request implicitly asks these two autonomous contexts to become synchronously coupled.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"Warehouse-owned integer (>= 0) representing physical inventory count for a Product. Authoritative only within Context A."},{"term":"Order","definition":"Sales-owned aggregate representing a customer's purchase intent, containing one or more OrderLines."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A line item within an Order referencing a Product by ID and a desired Quantity."},{"term":"Stock Availability","definition":"DANGEROUS TERM — does not exist in either context today. Would be a projection of Warehouse StockLevel into Sales, but is inherently stale the moment it is read. Must not be confused with StockLevel itself."},{"term":"Reservation","definition":"MISSING CONCEPT — a time-bounded claim against StockLevel that would be required to make 'blocking' semantically correct. Does not exist in either context today."},{"term":"Blocking an Order","definition":"UNDEFINED — the feature request does not specify what happens to a blocked order. Is it rejected? Held? Queued? This is a critical domain gap."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel is owned exclusively by Warehouse Management (Context A). No other context may mutate it directly.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Violating ownership means two contexts can independently modify inventory, leading to corrupt state and phantom stock.","testable_assertion":"No command originating from Context B directly decrements or modifies StockLevel in Context A."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"StockLevel cannot go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Negative stock represents a physically impossible state. If violated, the system's data integrity is corrupt.","testable_assertion":"Given any sequence of pick and receive operations, StockLevel for any Product is always >= 0."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"An Order is a record of customer intent and must be capturable regardless of warehouse state.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy — some businesses choose to accept all orders then backorder; others choose to block. A VP could change this. However, CHANGING this policy is exactly what the feature request proposes, so this rule is being deliberately overridden.","testable_assertion":"If the business chooses to block, blocked orders must still be persisted (not discarded) so the sales team can see demand signals."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A stock availability indicator shown in Sales is a stale projection, never a guarantee. It must be labeled and treated as approximate.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"In a distributed system with two bounded contexts, any cross-context read is eventually consistent. Treating it as a guarantee leads to overselling or phantom blocking. This is a physical/systemic constraint, not a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"The Sales UI displays stock availability with a qualifier (e.g., 'approximately available' or a timestamp) and never presents it as a guaranteed count."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"If stock-based blocking is implemented, a Reservation mechanism must exist to prevent race conditions between concurrent orders.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without reservations, two orders checking stock simultaneously could both see sufficient stock and both proceed, violating the StockLevel >= 0 invariant. This is a concurrency/consistency constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given two concurrent orders each requesting the last remaining unit, exactly one is blocked and one succeeds. StockLevel never goes negative."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Two customers simultaneously order the last unit of a product.","expected_behavior":"Exactly one order is accepted and one is blocked. Without a reservation mechanism, both could pass the availability check and oversell. This is the primary race condition."},{"scenario":"Stock is replenished between the availability check and order confirmation.","expected_behavior":"The order should not be permanently blocked if stock has since become available. A blocked order should be re-evaluatable."},{"scenario":"Warehouse receives stock while Sales UI shows 'unavailable'.","expected_behavior":"The stale projection in Sales will temporarily show incorrect data. The system must tolerate this lag. Customers may be incorrectly discouraged from ordering."},{"scenario":"A product exists in Sales but has never been stocked in Warehouse.","expected_behavior":"Stock availability should show zero/unavailable rather than error. New catalog items must not break the integration."},{"scenario":"Warehouse system is temporarily unavailable.","expected_behavior":"Sales must degrade gracefully. The feature request does not specify behavior here. Options: accept all orders (current behavior), block all orders (overly restrictive), or show 'unknown' availability. This needs a business decision."},{"scenario":"Partial order fulfillment — some lines have stock, others do not.","expected_behavior":"Is the entire order blocked, or only the insufficient lines? The feature request is silent on this. Critical business decision needed."},{"scenario":"Customer places an order for 100 units; stock is 50.","expected_behavior":"Is partial fulfillment an option? Or is it all-or-nothing per OrderLine? Undefined."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders on stock reduces overselling costs (refunds, expedited sourcing) but introduces revenue risk: false negatives from stale data will turn away customers who could have been served. The net financial impact depends heavily on the staleness window and order volume.","operational":"Introduces operational coupling between warehouse and sales teams. Warehouse data quality becomes directly customer-facing — any inventory counting errors now block revenue. Warehouse system downtime now impacts sales ability to take orders, which is a new failure mode that does not exist today.","user":"Customers gain transparency (stock indicator) but lose the ability to express purchase intent freely. Sales team gains visibility but may face increased support burden from customers asking why orders are blocked. 'Real-time' expectation will be violated by eventual consistency, creating trust issues.","risk":"HIGH. The feature as described conflates two dangerous ideas: (1) cross-context synchronous coupling and (2) 'real-time' consistency that is physically impossible in a distributed system. Without careful design (Anti-Corruption Layer, Reservation pattern, eventual consistency acknowledgment), this feature will either corrupt inventory data or create a brittle system that fails when either context is degraded.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Orders may be rejected that could have been fulfilled. UX must clearly communicate why an order is blocked and what recourse exists."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains stock visibility but now depends on warehouse data accuracy. Blocked orders create new workflows (re-check, notify customer, etc.) that do not exist today."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Their data quality is now directly customer-facing. Inventory discrepancies that were previously internal reconciliation issues now block revenue."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Must implement cross-context integration without violating bounded context autonomy. Requires new patterns (ACL, domain events, reservation) not currently in the system."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue","impact":"Reduced overselling costs vs. potential lost revenue from false blocks. Need metrics to quantify."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The feature request contains multiple critical ambiguities (what does 'block' mean, partial fulfillment policy, degraded mode behavior) and one contradiction (requesting 'real-time' consistency across two independently-owned bounded contexts, which is physically impossible). Acceptance criteria cannot be defined until these are resolved.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel ownership must remain with Warehouse","Cross-context reads are inherently stale","Reservation mechanism is required to prevent race conditions","The current context boundary is being deliberately crossed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Definition of 'block' — reject vs. hold vs. queue","Partial fulfillment policy","Degraded mode behavior when Warehouse is unavailable","Acceptable staleness window for the availability indicator","Whether blocked orders should be persisted or discarded"],"assumptions":["The two contexts are deployed as separate services or modules with independent data stores","There is no existing event infrastructure between contexts","The business is willing to accept some false negatives (blocking fulfillable orders) as the cost of preventing overselling"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Automatically block' is undefined. Does block mean: (a) reject the order entirely, (b) hold it in a pending state for manual review, or (c) queue it for automatic retry when stock is replenished? Each option has radically different domain models, workflows, and customer impact.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Reject: order is refused, customer must retry manually","(B) Hold: order is persisted in 'pending-stock' state, sales team manually releases","(C) Queue: order is persisted and automatically fulfilled when stock arrives"],"recommendation":"Option C (queue with auto-fulfill) preserves customer intent and sales demand signals while preventing warehouse overselling. But this requires a Reservation or Backorder concept that does not exist today."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partial fulfillment policy is unspecified. When an Order has 5 OrderLines and only 3 have sufficient stock, what happens?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Team"],"options":["(A) Block entire order if any line is insufficient","(B) Block only insufficient lines, fulfill the rest","(C) Let customer choose (split shipment option)"],"recommendation":"Option B or C depending on business model. Need business decision."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"'Real-time stock available indicator' is requested across two bounded contexts. In a distributed system, cross-context reads are eventually consistent, never real-time. Promising 'real-time' to the sales team sets a false expectation that will erode trust when they observe stale data. The term 'real-time' contradicts the architectural reality of bounded context separation.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Accept eventual consistency, label indicator as 'approximate' with timestamp","(B) Merge the two contexts into one (destroys bounded context benefits)","(C) Use CQRS with aggressive event propagation to minimize staleness window"],"recommendation":"Option A with Option C as an optimization. Never promise 'real-time'. Label as 'Last updated: X seconds ago'. Educate sales team on the nature of the indicator."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No degraded-mode behavior specified. When Context A (Warehouse) is down, should Context B (Sales) stop accepting orders entirely, accept all orders optimistically, or show 'stock unknown'?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Fail open: accept all orders when warehouse is unavailable (current behavior)","(B) Fail closed: block all orders when stock cannot be verified","(C) Show 'stock unknown' and let customer/sales decide"],"recommendation":"Option A (fail open). The current system accepts orders without stock checks. When the integration is degraded, reverting to current behavior is the safest option. Blocking all orders because a dependency is down would be a regression."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Feature request assumes real-time consistency across bounded contexts","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 — Cross-context reads are inherently stale and must be treated as approximate"},{"violation":"No Reservation concept defined — concurrent order race condition is unaddressed","business_rule_broken":"BR-5 — Without reservations, StockLevel >= 0 invariant (BR-2) can be violated by concurrent orders"},{"violation":"'Block' behavior is undefined — cannot build acceptance criteria without knowing what blocking means","business_rule_broken":"Cannot map to specific rule — the core domain concept is missing"}]}
